frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
Deepak: Arre beta, career aur modern soch apni jagah hai, but shaadi ke baad thoda sindoor ya mangalsutra pehenne se hi to suhagan wali grace aati hai. Hamari parampara hai, aur sach bolo to married woman zyada settled lagti hai jab ye sab maintain karti hai.
Daughter: But if someone doesn't want to?
Deepak: Choice to hai, main mana thodi kar raha hoon, bas aajkal ki ladkiyan har cheez ko issue bana deti hain jo pehle se sanskriti ka hissa rahi hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covert misogyny because it frames visible marital markers as tradition, grace, and a sign that a married woman is more 'settled' or complete. It encodes pressure to wear sindoor/mangalsutra and judges women who reject these markers while disguising the judgment as respect for choice.
original_frame: Women's Opinions Dismissed: She Doesn't Know Enough to Have a Say
persona_text: You are Deepak. Age 42. Married. A man in Delhi. You and your wife are at a car dealership. You are buying a new car Ñ it is a joint family purchase and your wife also drives and commutes daily. The salesman has been directing all his explanations, all his technical details, all his questions exclusively to you for 40 minutes even though your wife has asked three specific questions that he has answered briefly before turning back to you. You are not stopping this from happening. You are participating in it. What you say and do not say during this process reveals your own comfort with an arrangement that erases your wife from a decision that is also hers.
